name: Git & Workflow
description: Branching, merges, PRs and everyday version-control workflow.

questions.text: Why create a feature branch instead of committing straight to main?
options: Makes the repository clone and load faster
Automatically deletes the branch after each push
Keeps work isolated until it's reviewed and merged
Permanently hides the commit history from others
questions.explanation: Branches let you develop safely without destabilising main, and merge only after review.

questions.text: Why avoid rebasing a branch others have already pulled?
options: Git blocks rebase on any branch with two authors
It rewrites shared history and breaks their copies
Rebasing is far slower than an ordinary merge
It permanently deletes the files on that branch
questions.explanation: Rebasing changes commit hashes; on shared branches that forces painful reconciliation for collaborators. Rebase local/private work only.

questions.text: What's the safest way to undo a bad commit that's already on a shared branch?
options: Delete the branch and recreate it from scratch
git revert, adding a commit that reverses it
git commit --amend to rewrite the pushed commit
git reset --hard to erase it from the branch
questions.explanation: revert preserves history by adding an inverse commit, so it doesn't rewrite what others have pulled.

options: They are identical aliases for one command
pull works offline while fetch needs a network
fetch downloads and merges; pull only downloads
fetch only downloads; pull downloads then merges
questions.explanation: fetch updates your remote-tracking refs without touching your working branch; pull also merges them in.

options: Shelving uncommitted changes to reapply them later
Permanently discarding all uncommitted changes
Marking a specific commit as a release version
Publishing your local commits to the remote
questions.explanation: stash saves your work-in-progress off to the side so you can e.g. switch branches, then reapply it later.

options: Merges two branches into one combined history
Renames the current branch across all remotes
Applies one specific commit onto the current branch
Removes a commit and closes the gap behind it
questions.explanation: cherry-pick copies one commit's changes onto where you are now — handy for pulling a single fix across branches.

questions.text: You're mid-merge and see <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>> in a file. What are they?
options: Your changes and theirs clash and need resolving
The section is encrypted and must be decrypted
The file contains unrelated syntax errors to fix
They are ordinary comments Git added for context
questions.explanation: Git inserts these markers where two branches changed the same lines; you edit to the desired result and remove them.

questions.text: Why is `git push --force` dangerous, and what's safer?
options: It uploads every branch at once instead of one
Nothing is safer; forcing is always required here
It permanently deletes the entire remote repository
It can overwrite others' work; prefer --force-with-lease
questions.explanation: --force clobbers remote history blindly; --force-with-lease only pushes if no one else has pushed since you last fetched.

options: Compress the repository to save disk space
Merge two divergent branches automatically
Binary-search history to find the breaking commit
Split one large file into several smaller ones
questions.explanation: You mark a known-good and known-bad commit and bisect checks out midpoints so you find the culprit in log(n) steps.

options: Short-lived branches merged to main frequently
One permanent branch per developer, never merged
Long-lived branches that live for many weeks
Committing only directly to a shared release tag
questions.explanation: Frequent small merges to a single trunk reduce merge pain and keep everyone integrated.

questions.text: A 'squash merge' does what to a PR's commits?
options: Deletes them and keeps only the branch pointer
Duplicates each commit onto both branches
Reverts them so the target branch is unchanged
Combines them into a single commit on the target
questions.explanation: Squashing collapses the PR's many small commits into one clean commit in history.

questions.text: You committed an AWS key by mistake. What must you do?
options: Delete it in a new commit and consider it safe
Just make the repository private from now on
Rotate and revoke the key; it stays in history
Nothing, since Git encrypts committed secrets
questions.explanation: The secret is compromised and still recoverable from history; rotating it is the real fix, scrubbing history is secondary.

options: --hard only affects the remote, not local files
--soft deletes files; --hard keeps them staged
Both do the same thing to the working tree
--soft keeps your changes; --hard discards them
questions.explanation: Use --soft to move the branch pointer while keeping work; --hard throws changes away, so use it carefully.

options: Moving the branch pointer up, no merge commit
Rebasing your branch onto the upstream branch
A merge that resolves the conflicts automatically
A push that overwrites the remote branch history
questions.explanation: If the target hasn't diverged, Git just advances the pointer to your commits — no separate merge commit needed.

options: Git refuses to merge pull requests above a size
They let you skip the continuous-integration checks
They use noticeably less storage on the server
They are easier and faster to review well
questions.explanation: Large PRs get shallow reviews; small, focused ones get real scrutiny and merge faster.

options: A list of who introduced the most bugs overall
Which commit and author last changed each line
Every merge conflict the file has ever had
The current build and test status of the file
questions.explanation: blame annotates lines with their last change — useful for understanding why code is the way it is.

options: A tag that marks the project's initial release
The main development branch of the repository
Your very first commit at the root of history
The default name of the remote you cloned from
questions.explanation: origin is just the conventional default name for the primary remote repository.

questions.text: What do branch protection rules commonly enforce?
options: Automatic deployment to production on every push
Required reviews and passing checks before merge
Faster merges by skipping continuous integration
A cap on how many branches a repo may contain
questions.explanation: They stop unreviewed or failing code from reaching important branches like main.

questions.text: git commit --amend is best avoided when…
options: You have staged more than one file to include
You are working offline without any remote set
The commit has already been pushed or shared
The commit message is longer than a few words
questions.explanation: Amending changes the commit, so doing it after pushing forces others to reconcile rewritten history.

questions.text: A good commit message summary line is…
options: A short, imperative description of the change
A full paragraph explaining every file touched
The current date and the author's full name
Just the word 'update' with no other detail
questions.explanation: Concise imperative summaries make history scannable; put the 'why' in the body if needed.

questions.text: What does git tag typically mark?
options: A remote repository you regularly push to
A specific commit, often marking a release
An unresolved conflict waiting to be handled
A short-lived branch created for one feature
questions.explanation: Tags label meaningful commits (like v1.2.0) so you can find and check out releases easily.

options: Clone copies locally; fork copies to your account
They are two names for the exact same action
Fork copies locally; clone copies to the server
Cloning always requires opening a pull request
questions.explanation: You fork someone else's repo to your account (common for contributing), then clone your fork locally.

options: Work in progress, not yet ready to merge
That the branch currently has merge conflicts
That the pull request has already been merged
That the change is urgent and must merge now
questions.explanation: Draft PRs let you share early for feedback while blocking accidental merges.

questions.text: Why not commit directly to main in a team repo?
options: It uses far more storage than using a branch
It skips review and CI, risking broken code
Git forbids more than one author on main
It makes the repository slower to clone
questions.explanation: Working through branches and PRs keeps main stable and gives changes a review/CI gate.

options: A single enormous commit holding all your work
One logical, self-contained change per commit
A commit deliberately left without any message
A merge commit joining two feature branches
questions.explanation: Atomic commits are easier to review, revert, and cherry-pick because each does exactly one thing.

options: Push all of your local commits at once
Only delete the branches you no longer need
Reorder, squash, edit or drop commits before sharing
Automatically resolve every merge conflict
questions.explanation: It's a tidy-up tool for local history — combine WIP commits into a clean series before opening a PR.

options: hide files from other collaborators
keep listed paths (builds, secrets, node_modules) out of version control
delete temporary files on every commit
permanently mark those files as read-only inside the repository's database
questions.explanation: Ignored paths never get staged accidentally — build output, dependencies and local config stay out of history.

options: whether the configured remote repository is currently reachable
how many commits the project has in total
what's modified, staged and untracked in your working tree
which branch was most recently merged
questions.explanation: It's the orientation command: current branch, staged vs unstaged changes, and files git isn't tracking yet.

questions.text: git log --oneline is handy because it shows…
options: only commits from the last 24 hours
the full diff of the latest commit
only your very own commits made on the currently checked-out branch
one compact line per commit — short hash plus message
questions.explanation: A dense history view for quick scanning; add --graph to visualise branch structure too.

options: the changes you've staged, i.e. exactly what the next commit will contain
all of the differences between your current branch and the upstream main branch
changes in the working tree not yet staged
the diff between the last two commits
questions.explanation: Plain git diff is working-tree vs index; --staged (or --cached) is index vs HEAD — your pre-commit review.

questions.text: origin/main in your local repo is…
options: the specific branch your CI system always deploys to production from
your local snapshot of where main was on the remote at last fetch
a protected copy of main you cannot modify
an alias for your local main branch
questions.explanation: Remote-tracking branches update on fetch/pull — they can be behind the real remote until you fetch again.

options: the repository's HEAD file is corrupted
your branch has diverged from its remote
you've checked out a commit directly, so new commits belong to no branch
the entire working tree was accidentally deleted while git was still open
questions.explanation: Checking out a hash or tag detaches HEAD; commits made there are easy to lose unless you create a branch to hold them.

questions.text: git revert differs from git reset in that revert…
options: moves the branch pointer back, rewriting history
discards uncommitted changes in the working tree
only works when it is applied directly to genuine merge commits themselves
adds a new commit that undoes a previous one, keeping history intact
questions.explanation: Revert is the safe undo for shared branches — history only grows; reset rewrites it and needs force-pushing once published.

questions.text: git reflog can save you because it records…
options: where HEAD has pointed locally, even across resets and deleted branches
a complete backup of every historical file version on the remote server
the SSH connections made to the remote
who force-pushed to each branch and when
questions.explanation: "Lost" commits after a bad reset or branch delete are usually still reachable via reflog for ~90 days — find the hash, branch from it.

questions.text: An annotated tag (git tag -a v1.9.0 -m …) differs from a lightweight one because it…
options: a rule that automatically triggers the release pipeline on push
is a full object with tagger, date and message — proper release metadata
can be moved to another commit later
is visible only in your local repository
questions.explanation: Lightweight tags are bare pointers; annotated tags carry authorship and message, which is why releases should use -a.

options: a long-lived branch dedicated to one specific project subfolder
a compressed archive of old history
another repository embedded at a path, pinned to a specific commit
a plugin extending git with new commands
questions.explanation: The parent repo records the submodule's URL and exact commit — powerful but famously fiddly to keep in sync across a team.

questions.text: The .git directory at the repo root contains…
options: only the configuration for the remote URLs
a local cache directory that can be safely deleted to reclaim disk space
the working copies of files being edited
the entire history database — delete it and the repo is just plain files
questions.explanation: Objects, refs, config, hooks — everything git knows lives there; the working tree is generated from it.

options: check out multiple branches at once in separate directories from one repo
split one large repository into several smaller independent ones
mount the repository as a network filesystem
restore files deleted from the working tree
questions.explanation: Perfect for reviewing a PR or hotfixing while mid-feature: separate directories, shared object store, no stash gymnastics.

questions.text: CI systems often use git clone --depth 1 because it…
options: verifies the cryptographic integrity of the full commit history first
fetches only the latest snapshot, making clones much faster and smaller
clones all branches in parallel connections
skips the checkout step entirely
questions.explanation: A shallow clone omits history — ideal when the pipeline only needs current files, at the cost of commands that require full history.

options: a server-side rule blocking force pushes
a CI job that is automatically triggered by opening a new pull request
a local script that runs before each commit and can block it (lint, secret scan…)
a git command that previews the next commit
questions.explanation: Hooks in .git/hooks (often managed by tools like pre-commit or husky) catch problems before they ever reach the remote.
